---
title: "Gone Fishing at DuckLake"
subtitle: "shellfs · Go ring buffer · DuckLake · VersityGW · Arc · pequod REST API"
author:
  firstname: Markus
  lastname: Skyttner
  address: "Stockholm, Sverige"
  contacts:
    - icon: fa envelope
      text: markus.skyttner@redpill-linpro.com
      url: "mailto:markus.skyttner@redpill-linpro.com"
company-logo: redpill-linpro-logo.png
header-wave: redpill-linpro-wave.png
style:
  color-accent: "f03239"
  font-header: "Lato"
  font-text: "Lato"
format:
  revealjs:
    brand: _brand.yml
    embed-resources: true
    smooth-scroll: true
    toc: false
    logo: redpill-linpro-logo.png
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: ff.jpeg
  data-background-size: cover
  data-background-opacity: "0.4"
---

# Challenge

> Use Claude Code to develop a demo which ingests a Data Streams into a Data Lake and supports LLM-based time series analysis over MCP against the data

- A streaming data source is ingested into a DuckLake - "ps": use data about locally running processes on your host
- Use https://github.com/mskyttner/caddy-duckdb-module for adding REST and MCP to the data lake, enabling others to ...
- Use a .mcp.json to do LLM-supported time series analysis (using Claude Code)

# TLDR / Show Me: From `ps` to Sparkline

## Unix pipeline philosophy — one job each {.smaller}

> **Each step does exactly one thing and knows nothing about its neighbours.**

```
ps_arrow.sql          ingest-ps-arrow.sh          sparkline.sql
─────────────         ──────────────────          ─────────────
typed producer    →   transport bridge        →   lake consumer
                  │                           │
           Arrow IPC bytes              DuckLake events
           on stdout                    (any writer, any time)
```

| Role | Step | Knows about |
|------|--------|-------------|
| **Typed producer** | `ps_arrow.sql` | `ps` output format, Arrow IPC |
| **Transport bridge** | `ingest-ps-arrow.sh` | stdin bytes, DuckLake schema |
| **Lake consumer** | `sparkline.sql` | `events` table, textplot |

Each stage is independently runnable and replaceable:

- Swap `ps` for `vmstat` — only the producer changes
- Swap Arrow IPC for CSV or JSON — only the bridge changes
- Swap sparklines for a bar chart — only the consumer changes

The lake is the **contract** — a stable table that decouples how data arrives from how it is analysed.

## Step 1 — Capture: typed Arrow stream from `ps` {.smaller}

`ps_arrow.sql` — reads live process metrics via `shellfs`, creates a typed view, writes Arrow IPC to stdout:

```sql
-- ps_arrow.sql
LOAD shellfs;
LOAD arrow;

CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW ps_snapshot AS
WITH lines AS (
    SELECT unnest(string_split(content, E'\n')) AS line
    FROM read_text('ps -eo pid,pcpu,pmem,rss,comm --no-headers |')
),
fields AS (
    SELECT string_split_regex(trim(line), '\s+') AS f
    FROM lines WHERE trim(line) != ''
)
SELECT NOW() AS ts, f[1]::INTEGER AS pid, f[2]::DOUBLE AS cpu,
       f[3]::DOUBLE AS mem, f[4]::INTEGER AS rss, f[5] AS comm
FROM fields WHERE len(f) >= 5;

COPY (SELECT * FROM ps_snapshot) TO '/dev/stdout' (FORMAT ARROW);
```

```bash
duckdb --init .duckdbrc :memory: < ps_arrow.sql 2>/dev/null
# → binary Arrow IPC stream on stdout, schema enforced, ready to pipe
```

## Step 2 — Ingest: Arrow stream into DuckLake {.smaller}

`ingest-ps-arrow.sh` — pipes the Arrow stream into DuckLake via a second DuckDB process:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# ingest-ps-arrow.sh — one shot; wrap in a `while true; sleep 5; done` loop to poll

duckdb --init .duckdbrc :memory: < ps_arrow.sql 2>/dev/null \
  | duckdb --init .duckdbrc data/session.db \
    -c "LOAD arrow;
        SET ducklake_default_data_inlining_row_limit = 10000;
        ATTACH IF NOT EXISTS 'ducklake:data/catalog.db' AS lake (
            DATA_PATH 's3://iot-lake/data/'
        );
        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS lake.events (
            ts TIMESTAMPTZ, pid INTEGER, cpu DOUBLE,
            mem DOUBLE, rss INTEGER, comm VARCHAR
        );
        INSERT INTO lake.events SELECT * FROM read_arrow('cat - |');"
```

Two DuckDB processes: first enforces types and writes Arrow IPC bytes, second reads them from stdin via `read_arrow('cat - |')` and writes to the lake. SQL must be passed via `-c` so stdin carries Arrow bytes.

## Step 3 — Analyse: CPU sparklines from the lake {.smaller}

`sparkline.sql` — loads `textplot`, attaches the DuckLake catalog (read-only via `.duckdbrc`), renders per-process CPU trends:

```sql
-- sparkline.sql
INSTALL textplot FROM community;
LOAD textplot;

WITH bucketed AS (
    SELECT
        comm,
        time_bucket(INTERVAL '10 seconds', ts) AS bucket,
        AVG(cpu)                                AS avg_cpu
    FROM events
    WHERE ts >= NOW() - INTERVAL '5 minutes'
    GROUP BY comm, bucket
),
ranked AS (
    SELECT
        comm,
        AVG(avg_cpu)                                        AS overall_cpu,
        tp_sparkline(list(avg_cpu ORDER BY bucket),
                     width := 40,
                     mode  := 'absolute',
                     theme := 'utf8_blocks')                AS cpu_trend
    FROM bucketed
    GROUP BY comm
    HAVING COUNT(*) >= 2
)
SELECT printf('%-20s', comm) AS process,
       round(overall_cpu, 2) AS avg_cpu,
       cpu_trend
FROM ranked
ORDER BY overall_cpu DESC LIMIT 20;
```

```bash
duckdb --init .duckdbrc :memory: < sparkline.sql
# → process  avg_cpu  ▁▂▃▅▇▅▃▂▁▂▃▅▃▂▁▂▃▄▅▃▂▃▁▂▃▄▅▅▄▃▂▁▂▃▄▃▂▁
```

# Streaming into a Data Lake

## What do we actually want?

> Take a **fast, continuous data source** — logs, IoT sensors, process metrics — and land it in a **versioned, queryable lakehouse** with time travel, without an orchestration cluster.

Reduce complexity:

- **Single machine** — no Kafka, no Spark, no Kubernetes
- **Open formats only** — Parquet on S3, Arrow IPC in the pipe
- **SQL** — DuckDB SQL throughout
- **Local S3** — no cloud bill, no credentials to rotate

A stack that fits: duckdb + `shellfs` + a Go ring buffer + `DuckLake` for DataLake + `VersityGW` for S3 / object storage

## The components at a glance

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Linux command** (`ps`) | Data source — fast text stream |
| **Go ring buffer** | Temporal buffer — absorbs bursts, drains on demand |
| **DuckDB + `shellfs`** | Type gateway — enforces schema, writes to DuckLake |
| **DuckLake** | Lakehouse catalog — ACID, time travel, schema evolution |
| **VersityGW** | Local S3 — POSIX directory exposed as S3 API |
| **compact.sh** | Periodic DuckLake compaction — merges Parquet, expires snapshots |
| **pequod** | REST / MCP API — caddy-duckdb-module serving DuckLake data |
| **Arc** | Alternative hot-tier — 18M+ rec/s via HTTP ingest |

# The Storage Layer

## VersityGW — local S3 in one command {.smaller}

VersityGW translates S3 API calls to a local POSIX filesystem. No MinIO config, no Docker — one binary.

```bash
mkdir -p /tmp/lake-data /tmp/lake-iam

ROOT_ACCESS_KEY=demo ROOT_SECRET_KEY=demoSecret \
  versitygw --port :9000 --iam-dir /tmp/lake-iam posix /tmp/lake-data
```

```bash
# Create the bucket (once)
aws --endpoint-url http://localhost:9000 \
    --no-verify-ssl s3 mb s3://iot-lake --region us-east-1
```

Any S3-aware tool now speaks to `/tmp/lake-data/` — including DuckDB's `httpfs` and DuckLake.

- Apache-2.0 · Go · stateless (multiple instances → horizontal scale)
- Backends: POSIX filesystem, ScoutFS, Azure Blob, S3 proxy
- [github.com/versity/versitygw](https://github.com/versity/versitygw)

## DuckLake — catalog-first lakehouse on DuckDB 1.5.2 {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
DuckLake separates **catalog** (a local DuckDB file) from **data** (Parquet files in S3).

```sql
-- .duckdbrc — loaded automatically by duckdb --init
INSTALL ducklake FROM core;
INSTALL httpfs   FROM core;
LOAD ducklake;
LOAD httpfs;

CREATE OR REPLACE SECRET versity_s3 (
  TYPE S3,
  KEY_ID 'demo', SECRET 'demoSecret',
  ENDPOINT 'host.containers.internal:9000',
  URL_STYLE 'path', USE_SSL false,
  REGION 'us-east-1'
);
```

```sql
-- Attach catalog (local DuckDB file) → data lives in S3
ATTACH 'ducklake:data/catalog.db' AS lake (
    DATA_PATH 's3://iot-lake/data/'
);

CREATE TABLE lake.events (
  ts TIMESTAMPTZ, pid INTEGER,
  cpu DOUBLE, mem DOUBLE,
  rss INTEGER, comm VARCHAR
);
```
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
**What DuckLake gives you:**

- ACID transactions on S3
- **Time travel** — `AT (timestamp => ...)`
- Schema evolution
- Parquet compaction via `ducklake_merge_adjacent_files`
- Any S3-compatible backend

Every `INSERT` creates a new **snapshot** in the catalog — the foundation for time travel.

**Inlining** — small batches stay in the catalog DuckDB file (no Parquet round-trip) until compaction flushes them:

```sql
SET ducklake_default_data_inlining_row_limit = 10000;
```
:::
::::

# The Pipeline

## Architecture {.smaller}

```
[ps loop in container]
        │ raw text lines (CSV)
        ▼
[Go ring buffer  :9001]
  • absorbs bursts between 5-second DuckDB reads
  • GET /drain  →  header + lines, atomic clear
  • GET /health →  {"buffered":N,"capacity":10000}
        │
        │  every 5 seconds (ingest.sh)
        ▼
[duckdb  data/session.db]
  LOAD shellfs; LOAD ducklake;
  INSERT INTO lake.events
  SELECT NOW(), pid, cpu, mem, rss, comm
  FROM read_csv('curl -sf .../drain |', header=true, ...);
        │                              every 5 min (compact.sh)
        ▼                              ┌──────────────────────┐
[DuckLake catalog]                     │ ducklake_flush_inlined│
[data/catalog.db]  ──Parquet files──▶  │ ducklake_merge_files  │
                      [VersityGW :9000]│ ducklake_expire_snaps │
                      ./data/s3/       └──────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
                   [pequod  :8099]
                   caddy-duckdb REST/MCP API
                   ATTACH ducklake (READ_ONLY) → SELECT * FROM events
```

The ring buffer is **type-agnostic** — it holds raw text lines and knows nothing about schemas. Type enforcement happens entirely at the DuckDB `read_csv` boundary via `columns={}`.

## The Go ring buffer {.smaller}

A fixed-capacity circular buffer over stdin — no Arrow, no JSON, just strings.

```go
type RingBuffer struct {
    mu     sync.Mutex
    buf    [10_000]string
    head   int    // next write position
    count  int    // valid entries
    header string // first stdin line, kept as CSV header
}

func (r *RingBuffer) add(line string) {
    r.mu.Lock(); defer r.mu.Unlock()
    r.buf[r.head] = line
    r.head = (r.head + 1) % capacity
    if r.count < capacity { r.count++ }  // oldest silently overwritten when full
}
```

`/drain` reconstructs ordered lines and clears atomically:

```go
func (r *RingBuffer) drain() string {
    r.mu.Lock(); defer r.mu.Unlock()
    start := (r.head - r.count + capacity) % capacity
    // ... walk from start, join, reset head/count
}
```

- **Fixed array** — no allocations on `add`, O(1) everywhere
- **Atomic drain** — each record seen by DuckDB **exactly once** per 5-second tick
- HTTP `/health` endpoint for observability

## Connecting the source {.smaller}

**`vmstat`** — truly streaming, emits one row per second, header printed once:

```bash
vmstat 1 \
  | awk 'NR==1{next}
         NR==2{print "ts,r,b,swpd,free,buff,cache,si,so,bi,bo,in,cs,us,sy,id,wa,st"; next}
         {print strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ") "," $0}' \
  | ./bin/ringbuffer
```

**`ps`** loop — header emitted once before the loop, suppressed inside:

```bash
{
  echo "pid,cpu,mem,rss,comm"
  while true; do
    ps -eo pid,pcpu,pmem,rss,comm --no-headers \
      | awk '{printf "%s,%.1f,%.1f,%s,%s\n",$1,$2,$3,$4,$5}'
    sleep 1
  done
} | ./bin/ringbuffer
```

Both work because the ring buffer reads **line 1 as the header**, everything else as data.

## shellfs — shell commands as DuckDB file sources {.smaller}

The `shellfs` community extension makes any command output a valid file path in DuckDB SQL.

**Convention: trailing pipe character**

```sql
-- ✓ correct
SELECT * FROM read_csv('curl -sf http://localhost:9001/drain |', header=true);

-- ✗ wrong (this prefix does not exist)
SELECT * FROM read_csv('shell:curl ...');
```

The ingestion loop — type-safe, one DuckDB call per tick:

```bash
while true; do
  duckdb --init .duckdbrc data/session.db <<SQL
    SET ducklake_default_data_inlining_row_limit = 10000;
    ATTACH IF NOT EXISTS 'ducklake:data/catalog.db' AS lake (
        DATA_PATH 's3://iot-lake/data/'
    );
    CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS lake.events (
        ts TIMESTAMPTZ, pid INTEGER, cpu DOUBLE,
        mem DOUBLE, rss INTEGER, comm VARCHAR
    );
    INSERT INTO lake.events
    SELECT NOW(), pid, cpu, mem, rss, comm FROM read_csv(
      'curl -sf http://host.containers.internal:9001/drain |',
      header = true,
      columns = {
        pid: 'INTEGER', cpu: 'DOUBLE',
        mem: 'DOUBLE',  rss: 'INTEGER', comm: 'VARCHAR'
      }
    );
SQL
  sleep 5
done
```

Extensions (`shellfs`, `ducklake`, `httpfs`) and the S3 secret are pre-loaded from `.duckdbrc` — no `INSTALL` inside the loop.

## Arrow IPC variant — typed binary in the pipe {.smaller}

The `arrow` community extension adds `FORMAT ARROW` to `COPY` and `read_arrow()` to shellfs.

```bash
# Verified on DuckDB v1.5.2:
# COPY ... TO '/dev/stdout' (FORMAT ARROW)  → magic bytes FF FF FF FF ✓
# read_arrow('cat file.arrows |')           → types preserved ✓
```

Two-DuckDB pipe — makes the Arrow IPC stream observable and separates type-validation from the lake write:

```bash
while true; do
  duckdb :memory: "
    INSTALL arrow FROM community; LOAD arrow;
    INSTALL shellfs FROM community; LOAD shellfs;
    COPY (
      SELECT * FROM read_csv(
        'curl -sf http://host.containers.internal:9001/drain |',
        header=true, columns={pid:'INTEGER', cpu:'DOUBLE', ...})
    ) TO '/dev/stdout' (FORMAT ARROW);
  " 2>/dev/null \
  | duckdb --init .duckdbrc data/session.db "
    LOAD arrow;
    INSERT INTO lake.events SELECT * FROM read_arrow('cat - |');
  "
  sleep 5
done
```

Use the single-DuckDB form to start; add the pipe when you want Arrow IPC as an artifact or multiple sinks.

# Time Travel & Arc

## DuckLake time travel {.smaller}

Every `INSERT INTO lake.events` creates a **snapshot** in the catalog. Query any past state:

```sql
-- Current state
SELECT COUNT(*), AVG(cpu) FROM lake.events;

-- State 10 minutes ago
SELECT COUNT(*), AVG(cpu) FROM lake.events
  AT (timestamp => NOW() - INTERVAL '10 minutes');

-- Compare snapshots
WITH now   AS (SELECT AVG(cpu) avg FROM lake.events),
     then  AS (SELECT AVG(cpu) avg FROM lake.events
                 AT (timestamp => NOW() - INTERVAL '1 hour'))
SELECT now.avg - then.avg AS cpu_drift FROM now, then;
```

> **Arc writing Parquet directly to VersityGW does NOT give DuckLake time travel.**
> Arc bypasses the DuckLake catalog — no snapshot is created, so `AT (timestamp => ...)` has nothing to walk.

The fix: use Arc as a **hot tier** and the ingestion loop to copy from Arc's SQL API into DuckLake.

## Arc — hot tier for high-throughput ingest {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
[Arc](https://github.com/Basekick-Labs/arc) is a Go binary built on DuckDB + Parquet + Arrow. Single binary, S3-native, 18M+ rec/s via MessagePack HTTP ingest.

```toml
# arc.toml — point at VersityGW
[storage]
backend       = "s3"
s3_bucket     = "arc-stream"
s3_endpoint   = "127.0.0.1:9000"
s3_path_style = true
s3_use_ssl    = false

[ingest]
max_buffer_age_ms = 5000  # flush every 5s
```

```bash
# Generator pushes to Arc HTTP API
go run generator/main.go \
  | arc-send --url http://localhost:8000/ingest/iot/events
```
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
**Two-tier pattern:**

```
[Generator]
     │
     ▼
  [Arc :8000]          ← hot tier
  18M+ rec/s            seconds to hours
  Parquet → VersityGW
     │
     │ Arc SQL API
     ▼
  [DuckDB ingestion]   ← bridge
  INSERT INTO lake      every N minutes
     │
     ▼
  [DuckLake]           ← cold tier
  full time travel      days to years
```

Arc handles the intake rate that would overwhelm a 5-second DuckDB loop at production scale.
:::
::::

# Demo

## Startup sequence

Everything runs in containers via podman-compose:

```bash
make up        # builds image, creates data/s3 + data/iam, starts all services
make logs      # tail all service logs
make compact   # run compaction once on demand
make down      # stop and remove containers (data preserved)
```

Services started by `make up`:

| Service | Description |
|---|---|
| `versitygw` | Local S3 on `:9000` |
| `ringbuffer` | Go ring buffer on `:9001`, streams `ps` output |
| `setup` | One-shot: creates bucket + DuckLake table, then exits |
| `ingest` | Drains ring buffer into DuckLake every 5 seconds |
| `compact` | Compacts DuckLake every 5 minutes |
| `pequod` | REST/MCP API on `:8099` (caddy-duckdb-module) |

```bash
# Optional Arc hot-tier (opt-in profile)
make up-arc
```

## Querying the live lake {.smaller}

**Direct DuckDB** (catalog attach, supports time travel):

```sql
-- data/session.db already has the secret and ducklake loaded via .duckdbrc
ATTACH IF NOT EXISTS 'ducklake:data/catalog.db' AS lake (
    DATA_PATH 's3://iot-lake/data/'
);

-- Rolling 30-second CPU load per process
SELECT comm,
  time_bucket(INTERVAL '30 seconds', ts) AS bucket,
  AVG(cpu) AS avg_cpu, MAX(cpu) AS peak_cpu, COUNT(*) AS samples
FROM lake.events
WHERE ts > NOW() - INTERVAL '5 minutes'
GROUP BY comm, bucket ORDER BY bucket DESC, avg_cpu DESC LIMIT 20;

-- Time travel: CPU picture 2 minutes ago
SELECT comm, AVG(cpu) avg_cpu
FROM lake.events AT (timestamp => NOW() - INTERVAL '2 minutes')
GROUP BY comm ORDER BY avg_cpu DESC LIMIT 10;
```

**Via pequod REST API** (DuckLake attached at startup, `search_path = 'lake,main'`):

```bash
# Regular query — events resolves via search_path
curl -s http://localhost:8099/duckdb/query \
  -H "X-API-Key: <admin-key>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"sql": "SELECT comm, AVG(cpu) avg_cpu FROM events
       GROUP BY comm ORDER BY avg_cpu DESC LIMIT 10"}'

# Time travel — AT syntax works through the REST API too
curl -s http://localhost:8099/duckdb/query \
  -H "X-API-Key: <admin-key>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"sql": "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM events AT (timestamp => NOW() - INTERVAL '"'"'5 minutes'"'"')"}'
```

> `{"sql": "..."}` is the required key — not `"query"`. Use `X-API-Key` header. ATTACH runs once in `pequod-init.sql` at connection creation (not per-request), so each query starts with `SELECT` and routes through the read path correctly.

## Key findings — DuckDB v1.5.2

- **shellfs**: trailing pipe `'cmd |'` — not `'shell:cmd'`
- **Arrow IPC**: community extension — `INSTALL arrow FROM community`
- **`COPY TO '/dev/stdout' (FORMAT ARROW)`** works once arrow is loaded
- **`read_arrow('cmd |')`** works with shellfs — types survive the pipe
- **`.arrows`** = IPC streaming format (self-framing per batch, pipe-safe)
  **`.arrow`** = IPC file format (needs footer → not pipe-safe)
- **DuckLake time travel** requires writes through the DuckLake catalog — Arc writing Parquet directly bypasses it
- **DuckLake catalog is a local DuckDB file** — `ATTACH 'ducklake:data/catalog.db'`; data Parquet goes in S3
- **DuckLake inlining**: default row limit is only 10 rows; set `ducklake_default_data_inlining_row_limit = 10000` before ATTACH to keep small batches in the catalog without Parquet overhead
- **DuckLake in the Go driver**: `github.com/duckdb/duckdb-go/v2 v2.10502.0` embeds DuckDB 1.5.2 — same version as the CLI. Pre-mount the extension from your own image so `LOAD ducklake` works without a runtime download
- **ATTACH in init, not per-request**: putting `ATTACH` in `DUCKDB_INIT_FILE` runs once at connection creation; each query then starts with `SELECT` and routes through the read path. `SET search_path = 'lake,main'` lets callers write `FROM events` without schema qualification
- **Time travel works through the REST API**: `SELECT ... FROM events AT (timestamp => NOW() - INTERVAL '5 minutes')` — the `AT` clause is part of the `SELECT`, so it routes correctly
- **pequod query format**: `{"sql": "..."}` — not `{"query": "..."}`. Auth header is `X-API-Key: <key>` — not `Authorization: Bearer`. Admin role required for arbitrary SQL
- **podman 3.4.4 networking**: containers cannot resolve each other by hostname on the default bridge; use `host.containers.internal` (injected into every container's `/etc/hosts`) to reach port-mapped services on the host

## Thanks!

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Repo**

```
redpill-going-fishing/
├── ringbuffer/main.go      # Go ring buffer
├── ps_arrow.sql            # typed Arrow stream from ps
├── ingest-ps-arrow.sh      # pipe Arrow stream into DuckLake
├── sparkline.sql           # CPU sparklines via textplot
├── ingest.sh               # 5-second CSV DuckLake loop
├── compact.sh              # DuckLake compaction loop
├── pequod-init.sql         # REST API: DuckLake attach + search_path
├── Containerfile           # multi-stage image build
├── compose.yaml            # full stack definition
├── Makefile                # up / down / compact / test
└── index.qmd               # these slides
```

**Links**

- [DuckLake](https://ducklake.select/)
- [shellfs extension](https://github.com/rustyconover/duckdb-shellfs-extension)
- [arrow extension](https://duckdb.org/2025/05/23/arrow-ipc-support-in-duckdb)
- [VersityGW](https://github.com/versity/versitygw)
- [Arc](https://github.com/Basekick-Labs/arc)
- [textplot extension](https://github.com/query-farm/textplot)
- [caddy-duckdb-module](https://github.com/mskyttner/caddy-duckdb-module)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](redpill-linpro-logo.png){width="60%"}
:::
::::
